cit.q: in the neighbourhood when the machine for sugarcane resounds
Tēṉūr of the king rich in chariots, where the machine for sugarcane out-roars the elephant bulls
tīm piḻi yentiram pattal varunta
while the buckets for the nectar (tīm) pressing (piḻi, cf. Skt. √pīḍ) machines suffered [from emptiness]
entiram cilaikkum tuñcāk kampalai / vicayam aṭūum pukai cūḻ ālaitoṟum / karumpiṉ tīm cāṟu virumpiṉir micaimiṉ
You, who desired, enjoy the sweet juice of the sugarcane in all the halls surrounded by smoke, where the jaggery is being boiled, where the clamour does not cease, and where the machines resound!
karumpiṉ entiram ...
machine for sugarcane

note.p: A borrowing from Sanskrit yantra (NWS). Throughout the first millennium CE, authors consistently use it in the sense of machine or mechanism.

Its earliest meaning refers to a device associated with sugarcane pressing and the production of cane sugar, in connection with which Patiṟṟuppattu 19: 23 clarifies that this likely refers to a sugarcane press. It is telling that the early sugarcane mills are designated by ālai (from Skt. śālā, cf. Perumpāṇāṟṟuppaṭai, lines 260–262: entiram cilaikkum tuñcāk kampalai vicayam aṭūum pukai cūḻ ālaitoṟum karumpiṉ tīm cāṟu, Paṭṭiṉappālai lines 9–10: kārk karumpiṉ kamaḻ ālait tī 'the fire at the sheds fragrant from dark sugarcanes'; Cilappatikāram line 151: poṅk' aḻi ālaip pukaiyoṭum 'together with the smoke from the sheds of boiling and crushing' ), while the press is referred to by entiram (from Skt. yantra), which may reflect an Indo-Aryan linguistic connection underlying the technology.

The 41st tale (guḍaśālā; Vaidya 1958: 107–109) of the Avadānaśataka (ca. 2nd–4th century CE) provides one of the earliest textual attestations of organised sugarcane processing infrastructure. The story features sugarcane pressing-sheds (īkṣuśālaśatāni), lit. 'sugarcane halls', where cane was pressed (yatra cekṣuḥ pīḍyate), a dedicated press-house (yantraśālā), and a physician's prescription of sugarcane juice (vaidyenekṣurasa upadiṣṭaḥ) as a therapeutic remedy.

A possible influence behind these images may be traced to Buddhaghosa's Atthasālinī (commentary on the Dhammasaṅgaṇī), composed c. 430–450 CE at the Mahāvihāra monastery in Sri Lanka, which preserves probably the earliest surviving Pali account. At Atthasālinī 566 (Müller 1897, pp. 274–275,), the author describes machine-operators (yantavāhakā) leaving a village, spotting a ripe sugarcane field (ucchuvāṭa), and approaching the sugarcane owner (ucchusāmika) to announce their trade. The owner engages them with wages, fills the sugarcane shed (ucchusālā), and becomes their helper. They set up a cane-crushing machine (nāḷiyantaṃ) and the workers perform their respective tasks: the syrup-cooker (phāṇitapācaka) cooks the expressed juice, the jaggery-binder (guḷabandhaka) sets the product, and the owner weighs and distributes the shares (bhāga). The passage thus preserves the earliest surviving Pali account of an integrated industrial work, describing in sequence the pressing (pīḷana), the boiling of the juice (ucchurasa pakka), and the binding into jaggery (guḷa), and it shows that mobile teams of workers hiring themselves out by the shed-load were a recognised feature of the South Asian economy by at least the 5th century CE.

As for the attestations of a press named as yantra, another record may be the Bower Manuscript (5th–6th century CE) part II, fol. 6r, line 9, where we read: "One should obtain the fresh juice of yellow-fruit nightshade (nidigdhikā) by pressing it in a yantra" (nidigdhikāyāḥ svarasaṃ grāhayed yantrapīḍitam). Although Hoernle (1914, p. 95), in his revised translation, refers to it as a 'blunt instrument', it is likely that the term already denotes some kind of juice-pressing machine.

According to the mediaeval commentary on Patiṟṟuppattu 19: 23, Perumpāṇāṟṟuppaṭai 260–262, and Maturaikkāñci 258, the word ālai appears to have been used in the sense of 'press', although, as we have seen, it is etymologically derived from Skt. śālā ('hall') and originally denoted the building or shed housing the press, while the press/machine was designated by entiram (= Skt. yantra).
The Tivākaram cū. 2114 mentions entiram among other synonyms for poṟi, such as pulaṉ, ilāñcaṉai, and eḻutuviṉai.

cit.q: tiṅkaḷum nuḻaiyā entirap paṭu puḻai
entrance equipped with machines where not even the moon can enter
entirat takaippiṉ ampu uṭai vāyil
gates which possessed arrows in arrays of machines

sense.note: Elsewhere it refers to the mechanisms of fortress gates, where authors use it to denote fortification devices; possibly already reflecting Sanskrit influence (cf. Mahābhārata III. 268. 29; Rāmāyaṇa 6. 3. 15). The framework for these devices may be traced back to Arthaśāstra 2. 18. 5–6, which distinguishes between two categories of defensive machines employed in the protection or siege of fortifications: sthitayantrāṇi (stationary machines) and calayantrāṇi (mobile/moving machines).

cit.q: entirakkiṇaṟu
mechanised well
entiravāvi
mechanised pond

sense.note: Later, under the influence of Sanskrit literature, mechanised structures of palaces appear in the Maṇimēkalai: a mechanical well (entirakkiṇaṟu, 19: 102) and a mechanised pond (entiravāvi, 28: 7). These fall in the category of 'water-devices' (vāriyantrāṇi), discussed extensively in the 31st chapter (yantravidhāna) of the treatise called Samarāṅgaṇasūtradhāra, verses 107–172.

cit.ref: Anonymous mediaeval commentator on Patiṟṟuppattu 19, line 23 (ed. Cāminātaiyar 1903)
cit.q: entiram eṉṟatu ālaiyai.
entiram means ālai (sugarcane press).
entiram cilaikkum tuñcā kampalai vicayam aṭūum pukai cūḻ ālaitoṟum — ālaiyāravārikkum māṟāta ōcaiyaiyuṭaiya karuppañ cāṟṟaik kaṭṭiyākak kāyccum pukaicūḻnta koṭṭilkaṭōṟum. [ālai: ākupeyar]

entiram cilaikkum tuñcā kampalai vicayam aṭūum pukai cūḻ ālaitoṟum — every smoke-surrounded shed, possessing the unceasing sound of the roaring ālai (press), in which sugarcane juice is boiled into solid blocks/jaggery. [ālai is a metonymy].
karumpiṉ entiram ōtai — karumpiṟku iṭṭa ālaiyiṭattu ōcai
the sound at the place of the ālai (press) set up for sugarcane.

listBibl.bibl: Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1889. Pattuppāṭṭu mūlamum maturaiyāciriyar pārattuvāci nacciṉārkkiṉiyar uraiyum. 1st edition. Ceṉṉai: Tirāviṭaratnākara Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/s2jdk2wm5hp

Cāminātaiyar, U. Vē., ed. 1935. Puṟanāṉūṟu mūlamum uraiyum. Ceṉṉai: Lā Jarṉal Accukkūṭam. ark:/13960/t5dc0n26b

Hoernle, August Friedrich Rudolf. 1914. The Bower Manuscript: Facsimile Leaves, Nagari Transcript, Romanised Transliteration and English Translation with Notes. Calcutta: Archaeological Survey of India. (Archaeological Survey of India, New Imperial Series 22.)
Müller, Edward, ed. 1897. The Atthasālinī. London: Pali Text Society.
Vaidya, Paraśurāma Lakṣmaṇa, ed. 1958. Avadāna-śataka. Darbhanga: The Mithila Institute of Post-Graduate Studies and Research in Sanskrit Learning. (Buddhist Sanskrit Texts 19.)
